mnemonics: C.H.I.P.S. for E-Waste Sources: Computers, Home Appliances, IT Equipment, Phones/Mobiles, Stereos/Entertainment
Use the acronym H.A.R.D. to remember the hazardous materials: Heavy metals (like lead, mercury), Arsenic, Rare earth elements, Dioxins (from burning).
Think F.R.A.M.E. for E-Waste Types: Fridges/ACs (Large), RTVs/Monitors (CRT), Appliances (Small), Mobile/IT, Entertainment (Personal).
keyPoints: The two primary types of e-waste are ICT equipment (computers, phones) and Consumer appliances (fridges, microwaves).
Major sources include households (largest source), businesses, government agencies, and manufacturers (from defective units).
E-waste contains valuable recoverable materials like gold, silver, and copper, alongside hazardous substances like lead and mercury.
commonMistakes: Assuming only computers and phones are e-waste, ignoring large appliances like refrigerators and air conditioners.
Believing that dumping e-waste with regular municipal solid waste is an acceptable disposal method.
Overlooking that even small, everyday items like electric toothbrushes or calculators contribute to the e-waste stream.
